title: A Day as a Field Robotics Engineer at a Mining Company
setting: Rio Tinto autonomous haulage programme, Pilbara region WA (open-pit iron ore mine)
companySize: Large mining company (50,000+ employees globally, 40-person autonomous systems division)
description: Sarah Chen is a Field Robotics Engineer on Rio Tinto's autonomous haulage team in the Pilbara. She works on the systems that allow 240-tonne haul trucks to operate without drivers across a network of open-pit iron ore mines. Her role spans perception system maintenance, path planning refinement, and on-site troubleshooting when an autonomous truck behaves unexpectedly. She works a two-week on, one-week off FIFO rotation from Perth, splitting her on-site time between the operations centre (monitoring fleet behaviour, analysing logs) and the pit itself (inspecting sensors, testing software updates on parked trucks).
salary: $120,000 - $180,000 AUD per year (includes FIFO allowances and superannuation)

schedule.activity: Wake up at the mine camp, breakfast, review overnight fleet logs
schedule.note: Check if any autonomous trucks were taken out of service overnight. Two trucks flagged for LiDAR calibration drift — need to inspect today.

schedule.activity: Safety briefing and shift handover at the operations centre
tools: PPE: hard hat, hi-vis vest, steel caps, safety glasses
schedule.note: Review overnight incidents, planned blasting schedule (which affects truck routes), and any road changes the surveyors have flagged.

schedule.activity: Monitor autonomous fleet from the operations centre
schedule.note: Watch the fleet of 130+ autonomous trucks running their routes. Focus on the two flagged trucks — review their perception logs and localisation accuracy over the past 48 hours.

schedule.activity: Drive to the pit to inspect flagged trucks
schedule.note: One truck's front LiDAR has a damaged protective cover from a rock strike. Replace the cover and run a calibration routine. The second truck's issue is software — a SLAM parameter needs adjustment for a newly graded road section.

schedule.activity: Update SLAM maps for new road geometry
schedule.note: The mine surveyors graded a new haul road section overnight. Update the high-definition map that the autonomous trucks use for localisation, then validate the map against a reference survey drive.

schedule.activity: Test updated path planning on a parked truck
schedule.note: Before deploying the updated map to the full fleet, run the new map on a single truck in a controlled area. Walk alongside to verify it tracks the correct lane and stops at the right points.

schedule.activity: Lunch at the mine mess hall
schedule.note: Sit with the mine operations team. They want to extend autonomous haulage to a new pit — discuss the survey and mapping work that would be needed.

schedule.activity: Deploy map update to the fleet
schedule.note: Push the validated map update to all 130+ trucks via the over-the-air update system. Monitor the first trucks to traverse the new section and confirm they localise correctly.

Current project: improving the dust detection algorithm. Dust clouds from blasting and truck movements can confuse the LiDAR perception system, causing false obstacle detections and unnecessary emergency stops.

schedule.activity: Video call with Perth engineering team
schedule.note: Weekly sync: review the dust detection model's latest test results, discuss a new radar sensor trial for better dust penetration, plan next quarter's software release schedule.

schedule.activity: End of shift: update documentation, close permits, charge equipment
schedule.note: Write up the LiDAR repair and map update in the maintenance log. Prepare tomorrow's task list: radar sensor installation on a test truck.

schedule.activity: Shuttle back to camp, gym, dinner, downtime
schedule.note: FIFO routine: structured, well-catered, but remote. Call home, read a paper on radar-LiDAR fusion for mining, early lights-out for the 5 AM start.

responsibilities: Maintain and calibrate perception systems (LiDAR, radar, cameras, GPS) on a fleet of autonomous haul trucks
Update and validate high-definition maps used for truck localisation and path planning
Diagnose and resolve autonomous system faults in the field, often under time pressure from mine operations
Develop and test software improvements for perception, localisation, and path planning algorithms
Collaborate with mine operations, surveyors, and maintenance teams to integrate autonomous haulage into daily mine plans
Deploy software and map updates to the fleet via over-the-air systems and monitor rollout
Contribute to research and development projects that improve system robustness in harsh conditions
Ensure all robotic operations comply with mine site safety procedures and WA Mines Safety regulations
challenges: Dust, heat, and vibration — the Pilbara environment degrades sensors and electronics rapidly
The sim-to-real gap — software that works in simulation often fails in the pit due to conditions the simulator does not model (dust clouds, sun glare, wet roads after rare rain)
FIFO lifestyle and remoteness — two weeks on site with limited connectivity and distance from family
Coordinating with mine operations teams who need trucks running 24/7 and have limited patience for engineering downtime
Debugging intermittent faults on 240-tonne machines that cannot be easily brought to a workshop
Keeping a fleet of 130+ trucks consistently localised as the mine geometry changes daily from blasting and grading
rewards: Working on one of the largest autonomous vehicle deployments in the world — more autonomous trucks than most self-driving car companies have test vehicles
Directly measurable safety impact — autonomous haulage has significantly reduced injuries at mine sites
Excellent compensation and benefits typical of Australian mining sector FIFO roles
Solving hard robotics problems (perception in dust, localisation on changing terrain) that do not have textbook answers
Building rare expertise in industrial-scale autonomous systems — a skill set in growing demand across mining, energy, and logistics
The satisfaction of watching a 240-tonne truck navigate a complex mine site entirely on its own
careerPath: Junior Field Robotics Engineer → Senior Field Robotics Engineer → Lead Engineer, Autonomous Systems → Manager, Autonomous Haulage
Alternative: move to an autonomous vehicle company (Caterpillar, Komatsu, Waabi) as a perception or planning engineer
Alternative: transition to a robotics startup working on autonomous systems for agriculture, logistics, or construction
Alternative: specialise in safety-critical systems certification and become a functional safety engineer for autonomous machines
